---
title: "Stable Capability Fields"
chapter_id: "stable-capability-fields"
part_id: "foundations-alignment-governance"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-28"
primary_sources:
  - "scf"
  - "viea"
  - "talos"
  - "ladon_manhattan"
  - "moecot"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Four of five claim-source mappings now have reviewed passage references; `moecot` remains connector-only/source-note mapped until a durable local export or line-addressable connector capture is available."
  - "The stable_capability_field fixture validates schema/example consistency for field version, owner, qualification status, qualification lease status, evaluator independence, route scope, route permission effect, consumer policy, readiness gate refs, field history refs, default-route blockers, source refs, support-state effect, rollback obligations, and non-claims only; the readiness/residual gate harness now validates synthetic route-validity and rollback-readiness boundaries, but deployed route validation and rollback execution remain unimplemented."
  - "The implemented StableCapabilityFields Lean predicates cover finite replacement records only; they do not prove deployed route validation, evaluator integrity, or rollback readiness."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `stable-capability-fields` |
| Part | Part I - Foundations, Alignment, and Governance |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-28 |
| Primary source records | `scf`, `viea`, `talos`, `ladon_manhattan`, `moecot` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `scf`; supporting: `viea`, `talos`, `ladon_manhattan`; connector/recovery: `moecot` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `scf`, `viea`, `talos`, `ladon_manhattan`, `moecot`; passage-reviewed local raw cache: `scf`, `viea`, `talos`, `ladon_manhattan`; connector-only/source-note mapped: `moecot` |
| Test state | `stable_capability_field.valid.json` passes protocol fixture validation; `AsiStackProofs.StableCapabilityFields` builds locally; `python3 scripts/validate_readiness_residual_gates.py` validates synthetic route-validity and rollback-readiness boundaries. Deployed route validation, evaluator-integrity enforcement, and rollback execution remain planned. |

## Drafting guardrail

Stable Capability Fields are governed substitution boundaries. No SCF safety property is claimed implemented here beyond the schema fixture and any explicitly built proofs.

It follows governance rights because rights cannot survive implementation churn unless the capability boundary has a stable identity, stable authority ceiling, and explicit replacement policy.

This is the stack's first memory system for capability identity. Before the book can talk about replacing components, compiling repeated work into tools, or ratcheting benchmarks, it needs a way to say what must stay the same while implementations change. An SCF is that continuity record.

::: {.asi-human-only}
## Human Reading Path

Governance rights ask what must survive institutional change across long time horizons. Stable Capability Fields ask what must survive technical change. If a capability is replaced, the surrounding system needs to know what identity, authority, evidence, and rollback obligations stayed stable. A capability name is a promise only if the boundary behind the name is conserved.

This is the bridge from governance into recursive improvement. The stack can allow better implementations only if "better" does not mean the capability silently changed its job, widened its authority, or lost its audit history. The human version is simple: upgrades should improve the machinery without moving the terms of trust.

Continuity is the promise that lets replacement become progress rather than disguise.

The field is stable only if reviewers can see what remained the same. Stable identity is what lets improvement happen without losing the promises users relied on.

That continuity turns replacement into a reversible qualification problem: reviewers need the field identity, evidence history, and rollback obligations before a new implementation can inherit public trust.
:::

## Problem

Capability fields provide stable semantic boundaries so implementations can improve without changing what a capability means.

Rights become technical only when capability identity remains stable. Stable capability fields let the system improve components without moving the target under those rights. A capability must have a stable meaning before it can be routed to, qualified, replaced, rolled back, or governed. Otherwise every implementation change is also a semantic change, and no one can tell whether the system improved or merely redefined success.

SCF supplies the core distinction: a field is the durable semantic boundary; an implementation is a replaceable realization of that boundary. The field carries identity, interface, authority ceiling, evaluator policy, qualification context, route validity, state-migration obligations, evidence, lifecycle state, and recovery paths. That separation is what lets recursive improvement be bounded instead of ambient.

Capability names should not be allowed to drift faster than evidence. If "planner," "retriever," "verifier," or "runtime adapter" means a different authority surface after every upgrade, the rest of the stack cannot reason about safety, regressions, or replacement. The field fixes the question before candidates compete to answer it, and that fixed question is what lets governance rights survive technical churn.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Plugin replacement and model swapping do not define identity, authority, qualification evidence, or rollback semantics.

Replacing a model, tool, prompt, adapter, or specialist can look like an ordinary upgrade while changing the actual capability. A faster implementation may drop edge cases. A stronger model may gain broader authority through a route that was only meant for a narrow field. A new evaluator may be easier to satisfy. A migration may preserve outputs while corrupting hidden state. None of these failures is visible if the system treats "use the new one" as the whole upgrade protocol.

SCF makes the upgrade question testable: does this candidate satisfy the same field contract, under this qualification context, without expanding authority, losing regressions, capturing the evaluator, or destroying rollback?

Without that test, a system can suffer capability amnesia. It remembers that a component was replaced, but not what the old component was obligated to preserve, which regressions mattered, which authority ceiling applied, or which evaluator was trusted at the time. SCFs keep those obligations attached to the field rather than scattered across release notes.

The most dangerous upgrade failures can look successful locally. The new implementation may pass its own demo while forgetting the field's historical incidents, weakening an audit hook, changing the meaning of a route, or treating an expired qualification as fresh. A stable field makes those losses visible as boundary violations instead of hidden costs of progress.

## Core Claim

[stable-capability-fields.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] A stable capability field is a governed boundary with identity, interface, authority ceiling, qualification evidence, and rollback policy.

The claim remains at `argument` support. SCF supplies reviewed passage support for field identity, qualification, route validation, authority ceilings, lifecycle, state migration, and rollback vocabulary. VIEA supplies reviewed artifact, support-state, residual, regression, routing, and permission-envelope context. Talos supplies reviewed job, evidence, proof-bundle, replay, and uncertainty context. Ladon/Manhattan supplies reviewed handle, permission-check, controlled-injection, and compartment context. `moecot` remains connector-only/source-note mapped. Source-reported executable fragments, runtime behavior, or benchmark claims are not treated as locally verified.

### Claim-source mapping status

Appendix C maps this core SCF claim to every assigned source note. Four mappings now carry reviewed passage references; `moecot` remains connector-only/source-note mapped. The mappings support the governed-boundary vocabulary for fields, interfaces, authority ceilings, qualification, evidence, routing, and rollback, not a claim that deployed route validation or evaluator integrity has been proven.

| Source | What it supports | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| `scf` | Stable field identity, versioned contracts, content-bound implementation artifacts, qualification claims, evaluator policy, route validation, lifecycle states, authority ceilings, and recovery paths. | Does not prove production safety, global alignment, strategic-deception resistance, or deployed rollback behavior. |
| `viea` | Durable artifacts, support states, residuals, regression coverage, routing, verification ledgers, and intent-to-execution handoff boundaries around capability use. | Architecture proposal only; no completed VIEA deployment or benchmark/runtime result is proven here. |
| `talos` | Typed cognitive jobs, deterministic/auditable execution, evidence-bound artifacts, verification states, proof bundles, replay, mediated external access, and residual uncertainty around capability invocation. | Design source only; execution-security, approval behavior, promotion behavior, and benchmark behavior are not reproduced in this repo. |
| `ladon_manhattan` | Authority-handle pressure for capabilities touching secrets or privileged actions: constrained handles, permission checks, policy boundaries, compartments, and controlled injection points. | Security architecture/specification only; no kernel implementation, side-channel validation, audit-log implementation, or security audit is claimed. |
| `moecot` | Compact orchestration, specialist lanes, fail-closed ledgers, readiness gates, replay, and promotion blockers around routed capabilities. | Connector-readable implementation-reference context only; source-reported runtime and benchmark claims are not reproduced or inspected here. |

## Mechanism

An SCF is not just a registry row. It is the contract that lets a system ask three questions separately: what capability is being requested, which implementation may currently satisfy it, and under what evidence and authority may that implementation be routed to. That separation prevents a stronger candidate from quietly inheriting a weaker candidate's trust envelope.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Field contract: identity + interface"] --> B["Candidate implementation artifact"]
  A --> C["Authority ceiling + evaluator policy"]
  B --> D["Scoped qualification context"]
  C --> E["Narrow route validator"]
  D --> E
  P["Route proposer (untrusted)"] --> E
  E --> F{"Same field, valid route?"}
  F -- "yes" --> G["Lifecycle: shadow / canary / qualified / default"]
  F -- "no" --> H["Reject / quarantine / residual"]
  G --> I["Evidence + regression ledger"]
  H --> I
  I --> J["Rollback and recovery path"]
```

**How to read the capability field path:** The SCF path separates the field contract from candidate implementations. A route proposer may nominate a candidate, but the validator checks field identity, authority, qualification context, lifecycle state, evidence, and rollback before any implementation becomes default.

The field record separates identity from implementation. Qualification claims bind to exact artifacts and scoped contexts rather than to a generic model or tool name. Lifecycle states such as shadow, canary, qualified, default, deprecated, and retired make promotion reversible and inspectable. Route validity and evaluator integrity are therefore prerequisites for default use, not optional paperwork after the fact.

Qualification is scoped, not global. A candidate can be qualified for one domain, risk tier, authority ceiling, hardware context, benchmark epoch, or user population without being qualified everywhere. Its evidence should be append-only enough that later readers can reconstruct what was known when it was promoted.

Qualification should also age. A candidate qualified under one benchmark epoch, source corpus, hardware profile, permission tier, or threat model should not remain default forever by inertia. The field record should make review intervals, expiration conditions, incident triggers, requalification duties, and lease status visible so stability does not become stagnation. The qualification lease is the machine-readable version of that rule: it says which epoch qualified the implementation, whether that lease is active, stale, revoked, or fixture-only, when the route must be reviewed, and what events force requalification before default use continues.

The route proposer may be broad and heuristic; the route validator must be narrow and explicit. It checks field identity, claims, leases, profiles, grants, state paths, composition certificates, and authority ceilings before the candidate can become the default route. That split keeps creative discovery from becoming self-authorization: broad search may nominate a route, but a narrow validator decides whether the route still belongs to the field.

## Interfaces

The field record has different faces for different layers. Planning sees a semantic capability boundary. Execution sees an authorized route. Evidence sees qualification claims and regressions. Governance sees authority ceilings, evaluator policy, lifecycle state, incidents, and recovery paths.

- Planning requests field capabilities.
- Execution invokes authorized routes.
- Evidence records qualification and regression results.

Minimum fields:

- `field_id`
- `field_version`
- `owner`
- `semantic_boundary`
- `interface_contract`
- `authority_ceiling`
- `implementations`
- `lifecycle_state`
- `qualification_context`
- `qualification_status`
- `qualification_lease`
- `qualification_lease_status`
- `qualification_predicates`
- `evaluator_policy`
- `evaluator_independence`
- `route_validity`
- `route_scope`
- `route_permission_effect`
- `consumer_policy`
- `evidence_refs`
- `readiness_gate_refs`
- `field_history_refs`
- `incident_refs`
- `review_triggers`
- `state_migration`
- `regression_suite`
- `rollback_plan`
- `rollback_obligations`
- `default_route_blockers`
- `source_refs`
- `support_state_effect`
- `non_claims`

Planning requests a field by semantic boundary and capability profile. Execution invokes only authorized routes. Evidence records qualification, regressions, incidents, appeals, and lifecycle transitions. Governance decides whether a field can broaden authority or change evaluator policy.

The same field record becomes an anchor for procedural memory and benchmark ratchets. A generated tool should know which field it is trying to satisfy. A benchmark result should know which field boundary it evaluates. A replacement transaction should know which field authority ceiling it may not silently widen. A readiness gate should know whether the field's qualification lease is still fresh or has become stale evidence. A router should read default-route blockers before treating a canary or residual route as qualified for ordinary use.

## Invariants

- Replacement cannot expand authority by default.
- Evaluator integrity is protected.
- Qualification context is explicit and time-bound.
- Rollback remains available after failed mutation.
- Qualification leases expire or downgrade when their review triggers fire.

Identity binding is the field's strongest invariant: replacing an implementation must not change the field's semantic boundary unless governance explicitly creates a new field or version. Authority is also bound to the field, not inherited from the raw power of the candidate implementation.

Evidence locality keeps qualification from traveling by name alone. Qualification evidence belongs to a field, an implementation artifact, and a context together. Moving one of those three pieces without rechecking the others should downgrade the route to shadow, canary, or residual rather than preserving default status by name.

## Failure modes

- Field identity drift.
- Evaluator capture.
- Authority expansion during replacement.

Field identity drift happens when the name stays stable while the behavior changes. Evaluator capture happens when the candidate influences the policy or measurement that qualifies it. Authority expansion happens when a more capable implementation inherits a route that was safe only for a weaker one. Each should block default promotion until a replacement transaction records the residual and review path.

The quiet failure is orphaned regression memory. A field may accumulate tests and incidents over time, but an upgrade can leave them behind if the suite is stored with an implementation instead of the field. The SCF should keep regression floors at the field boundary so future candidates inherit the obligation to preserve them.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The first SCF implementation surface is the existing `stable_capability_field` schema expanded with field version, owner, lifecycle, qualification context/status, qualification lease/status, evaluator policy, evaluator independence, route validity/scope, route permission effect, consumer policy, evidence references, readiness gate refs, field history refs, incidents, review triggers, state migration, regression suite, rollback plan, rollback obligations, default-route blockers, source refs, support-state effect, and non-claims. Schema validation confirms record shape only; candidate qualification still depends on evidence, lease freshness, evaluator independence, and preserved regressions.

A field history fixture should make identity, lifecycle, and evidence aging concrete: one field, two candidate implementations, one preserved regression, one failed canary, one expired qualification lease, one incident-triggered review, and one rollback path. That fixture would not claim a deployed route validator.

## Beyond the State of the Art

Stable capability fields give the stack a capability-identity and replacement market. It lets implementations improve, migrate, split, merge, retire, or roll back while the consuming system continues to know which capability is qualified, under what authority, with which evidence and residuals.

A stable capability field preserves identity, interface, authority ceiling, qualification evidence, regression memory, and rollback policy while implementations change. Field identity stays separate from implementation, qualification claims bind to exact artifacts, lifecycle transitions such as shadow, canary, qualified, default, deprecated, and retired remain explicit, and promotion requires route validity plus evaluator integrity.

Planning requests field capabilities, execution invokes authorized routes, and evidence records qualification and regression results. Replacement cannot expand authority by default, evaluator integrity is protected, qualification context is explicit and time-bound, and rollback remains available after a failed mutation. Identity drift, evaluator capture, and authority expansion during replacement should block qualification, force requalification, trigger route rollback, or leave residual records before the field remains default.

This capability-identity market is still a proposed control layer. SCF support should remain `argument` until lifecycle-transition fixtures, qualification records, evaluator-integrity checks, rollback-route tests, authority non-expansion predicates, and regression histories show that implementations can change without changing the field's trust contract.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Stable capability field fixture validation | Check that the SCF fixture matches the public schema, including field version, owner, qualification status, qualification lease status, evaluator independence, route scope, route permission effect, consumer policy, readiness refs, field history refs, default-route blockers, source refs, support-state effect, rollback obligations, and non-claims. | implemented by protocol validation; validated locally |
| Qualification predicate finite-record proof | Check that a replacement record is allowed only when the candidate satisfies the field qualification predicate. | implemented in `AsiStackProofs.StableCapabilityFields`; runtime qualification test not run |
| Route validity test | Check that route grants, profile claims, leases, and state paths are valid for the field. | implemented by readiness/residual gate harness for synthetic route/gate/replacement records; deployed route validation not run |
| Authority non-escalation finite-record proof | Check that authority-expanding replacement is rejected when no governance grant is present. | implemented in `AsiStackProofs.StableCapabilityFields`; runtime route enforcement not run |
| Rollback readiness test | Check that recovery and state-migration paths exist before default promotion. | implemented by readiness/residual gate harness for rollback receipt and monitor-state requirements; deployed rollback not run |

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:scf.field_identity.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.StableCapabilityFields` | An implementation can replace another only if it satisfies the field qualification predicate. | implemented |
| `lean:scf.field_identity.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.StableCapabilityFields` | A replacement that expands authority without a governance grant is rejected. | implemented |

These proof targets are implemented only over finite Lean replacement records in `AsiStackProofs.StableCapabilityFields`. They prove that an allowed replacement record carries the field qualification predicate, and that a replacement requesting authority above the field ceiling is rejected when no governance grant is present. They do not prove route validity, evaluator integrity, rollback readiness, or deployed lifecycle enforcement.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `scf` | Stable Capability Fields | governance_recursive_self_improvement | Use public release v1.0 when available. Stable boundaries, replacement, bounded authority, recoverable evolution. | source note available; local raw cache available; passage reviewed for field identity, qualification, route validation, authority, lifecycle, and rollback vocabulary |
| `viea` | Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture | whole_stack_execution_spine | Keystone source. Human intent -> command contracts -> artifacts -> routing -> runtime targets -> verification -> deployment -> feedback. | source note available; local raw cache available; passage reviewed for artifact graphs, support states, residuals, regressions, routing, and permission envelopes |
| `talos` | Talos Protocol | labor_execution_os | AI labor OS. Deterministic cognitive manufacturing, typed jobs, control planes, auditability, tool isolation. | source note available; local raw cache available; passage reviewed for typed jobs, evidence-bound artifacts, proof bundles, replay, and residual uncertainty |
| `ladon_manhattan` | Ladon & The Manhattan Protocol | security_governance | Kernel-level security architecture for high-agency AI. | source note available; local raw cache available; passage reviewed for handles, permission lookup, controlled injection, and Digital SCIF isolation |
| `moecot` | MoECOT-Agent Architecture Whitepaper | implementation_reference | Concrete implementation evidence: governed low-parameter multi-core runtime, readiness gates, ledgers, replay. | source note available; connector-only/source-note mapped |

The SCF source set supports field identity and lifecycle vocabulary while preserving the distinction between reviewed local passages, connector-only implementation-reference context, schema validation, finite Lean predicates, and actual deployed route behavior.

## Summary

Stable Capability Fields are upgrade sockets for governed intelligence. They let a capability improve by changing implementations while preserving semantic identity, authority ceilings, evaluator policy, evidence, route validity, and rollback.

Stable fields make self-improvement possible without making every upgrade a constitutional event. Field updates become explicit replacement transactions. The field says what must remain true; the replacement layer says how a candidate proves it remained true before promotion.

Later learning and routing layers depend on this discipline. Procedural memory needs SCFs so new tools inherit a field boundary rather than becoming free-floating automation. Benchmark ratchets need SCFs so evidence improves a named capability instead of a vague sense of progress. Policy optimization needs SCFs so reward pressure updates behavior only inside a field whose authority and rollback remain visible. Without stable fields, self-improvement collapses into replacement by anecdote: a new component looks better, a few examples pass, and the system silently forgets what identity, permission, evaluator independence, and fallback were supposed to remain true.

## Handoff

Stable fields define what must remain true across implementation changes; Capability Replacement and Rollback defines how a candidate earns the right to replace the current implementation. Durable identity becomes a replacement transaction: qualification evidence, regression floors, residual escrow, monitor windows, rollback ownership, and authority deltas decide whether improvement can become a governed state change.
